nativeRange.habitat: Found on reef crests and slopes with rich coral growth, typically at depths of 1–15 m. Wild specimens tend to associate closely with sponges and tunicates.

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on microalgae and vegetable matter; has been documented accepting frozen and dried foods containing marine algae and spirulina. Tends to pick at algae growth on live rock and tank surfaces throughout the day.

avoidWith: other Ecsenius blennies unless tank is large

imageCandidates.notes: Photo (c) portioid; ~1024px large. Sharp, whole fish in classic curved perch pose on coral; clearly shows the two diagnostic dark belly spots, ornate black/orange facial markings and white lower head.
Photo (c) portioid; ~1024px large. Crisp side profile of a vibrant live individual, two dark belly spots and ornate eye markings clearly visible; different angle for variety.
Photo (c) portioid; ~1024px large. In-focus individual on green coral, two dark spots and facial pattern well shown; slight green cast, kept as variety extra.

summary: A small reef blenny from the Philippines and Malaysia, the Two Spot Bimaculatus Blenny is generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe. It tends to perch on rocks and graze on microalgae throughout the day, and is recognized by two distinctive dark spots near the belly.
careNotes: The Two Spot Bimaculatus Blenny — also sold as the Bimaculatus Blenny or Two Spot Blenny — is a small combtooth blenny native to the Western Pacific, found in the Philippines and northeast Borneo (Sabah), Malaysia. Adults reach a maximum of around 4.5 cm, making this one of the smaller blennies commonly available in the marine hobby. In the wild it is generally observed perching on and around rocks, sponges, and tunicates on reef crests and slopes at depths of 1–15 m; in aquariums it tends to occupy the bottom and lower rockwork zones, grazing on microalgae throughout the day. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised, as blennies in this group have been known to jump from open-top tanks. It tends to be territorial toward conspecifics and other members of the genus Ecsenius, particularly in smaller systems; one specimen per tank is generally recommended unless the aquarium is large enough to provide clearly separated territories. It is widely considered reef-safe and has been kept successfully alongside corals and invertebrates. As an algae grazer that readily accepts prepared foods, it is considered a good fit for beginners to the marine hobby. Breeding in captivity has been documented — the species is oviparous, with distinct pairing and demersal adhesive eggs — but successful spawning is rarely achieved in hobbyist aquariums.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; eggs are demersal and adhesive, attached to substrate. Captive breeding is rarely achieved in hobbyist aquariums.

additional.notes: Cross-checked size (2 in vs LiveAquaria 1.5 in), tank size (24 gal vs 10 gal), and care level (intermediate vs easy). Averaged adult size; biased toward larger tank recommendation per playbook.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; demersal adhesive eggs. Rarely bred in captivity by hobbyists.
